answer: Concurrent resolution handling: 1) Optimistic locking: error record has version field, 2) Resolution begins: read error with current version, 3) Apply resolution logic, 4) Update error with WHERE id = ? AND version = readVersion, 5) If version mismatch (concurrent update), database returns 0 rows updated, 6) Throw ConcurrentModificationException, 7) Client receives 409 Conflict, retries with latest error state, 8) Alternative: pessimistic locking with SELECT FOR UPDATE (locks row during resolution), 9) Resolution status check: if error already RESOLVED, skip resolution and return success (idempotent), 10) Audit log captures all resolution attempts (including conflicts). Prevents: duplicate resolution actions, race conditions causing incorrect state. User sees: Error already resolved by User X at [timestamp].

question: How does the progress tracking checkpoint system work for large batch processing?
answer: Progress checkpoint system: 1) Large batch (50k records) divided into checkpoints (every 5k records), 2) Session record stores: lastCheckpoint (record offset), processedCount, errorCount, 3) After each checkpoint: update session with current progress, commit transaction, 4) On failure or pause: resume from lastCheckpoint (not from start), 5) Checkpoint data: { checkpoint: 3, offset: 15000, processedCount: 14750, errorCount: 250, timestamp }, 6) Resume logic: query staging WHERE processingStatus IN (pending, validated) AND offset > lastCheckpoint, 7) Benefits: resume after crash without reprocessing, accurate progress reporting, incremental success (partial completion better than nothing), 8) UI shows: Processing checkpoint 8 of 10 (40,000 / 50,000 records). Checkpoints prevent losing hours of work on near-completion failures.

question: What queue infrastructure does AndroidSyncProcessor use to route InventoryCheck events to the staging queue?
answer: AndroidSyncProcessor queue routing: 1) BullMQ queue infrastructure with Redis backend, 2) AndroidSyncProcessor handles android-sync-processing queue, 3) On InventoryCheck event detection: check event type, create job data for staging queue, add job to inventory-check-staging queue, 4) Job routing: InjectQueue(inventory-check-staging), queue.add(process-inventory-check, jobData), 5) Queue communication: android-sync-processing -> inventory-check-staging via BullMQ job creation, 6) Job data includes: sessionId, barcode, location, timestamp, correlationId, 7) Staging processor picks up job asynchronously, 8) Benefits: decoupling, parallel processing, failure isolation. Queue names defined in QUEUE_NAMES constant. Routing enables specialized processors for different event types.

question: How does the LoggerModule provide structured logging across the backend?
answer: LoggerModule structured logging: 1) Winston logger or NestJS Logger with custom transports, 2) Log format: JSON with fields: timestamp, level, message, context, correlationId, userId, metadata, 3) Injection: services inject Logger, call logger.log(message, context), 4) Context includes: service name, method name, request ID, 5) Transports: console (development), file (production), external service (Datadog, CloudWatch), 6) Log levels: error, warn, info, debug, verbose, 7) Configuration: log level set per environment (debug in dev, info in prod), 8) Request middleware adds correlationId to all logs in request scope, 9) Structured logs enable: searching by correlationId, filtering by context, parsing for analytics. All logs JSON-formatted for machine parsing.

question: What is the purpose of the AgentModule and how does it relate to AI functionality?
answer: AgentModule provides AI-powered warehouse assistance: 1) LangChain agent for natural language queries about inventory, 2) Agent tools: query barrels, search lots, find locations, analyze trends, 3) User asks: Where are all barrels from lot ABC123?, agent queries database and returns natural language answer, 4) Integration with OpenAI GPT-4 for language understanding, 5) Prompt engineering for warehouse domain knowledge, 6) Tool definitions: getBarrelsByLot, getLocationOccupancy, getCustomerInventory, 7) Agent configuration: AGENT_MAX_ITERATIONS env var limits reasoning steps, 8) Use cases: chatbot for warehouse staff, automated reporting, anomaly detection. AgentModule exports AgentService used by chatbot UI and scheduled analysis jobs.

question: How does the frontend chatbot component architecture support AI-powered warehouse assistance?
answer: Frontend chatbot architecture: 1) React component with chat UI (messages list, input field), 2) State management: messages array, loading state, error state, 3) API integration: POST /api/agent/chat with user message, 4) Streaming response: SSE or chunked transfer for real-time agent responses, 5) Message types: user, assistant, system, error, 6) Markdown rendering for formatted responses (tables, lists, code blocks), 7) Context management: conversation history sent with each message (last 10 messages), 8) Features: copy response, regenerate, clear conversation, suggested prompts, 9) UI shows: thinking indicator while agent processes, tool calls made by agent (Querying barrels...), 10) Persistence: conversation saved to localStorage, optional save to database. Enables natural language inventory queries without SQL knowledge.

question: What business rules govern ownership type normalization, and when should strict mode vs tolerant mode be used?
answer: Ownership type normalization rules: 1) Input types: FULL, PARTIAL, HOUSE, CUSTOMER, various spellings/cases, 2) Normalization: trim whitespace, uppercase, map variants (Full -> FULL, partial -> PARTIAL), 3) Strict mode: reject unrecognized types with validation error, used for new data entry (prevent typos), 4) Tolerant mode: attempt to normalize, fallback to OTHER or default type, used for legacy data import (accept historical variations), 5) Type mapping: Full/100%/Whole -> FULL, Part/Partial/Split -> PARTIAL, House/Warehouse -> HOUSE, 6) Validation: check against allowed types enum, 7) Mode selection: strict for user input (API validation), tolerant for batch imports (CSV uploads). Normalization ensures consistent reporting despite input variation.

question: What constraints prevent overlapping barrel ownerships?
answer: Overlapping ownership prevention: 1) Temporal constraint: only one active ownership per barrel-customer pair at a time, 2) Check constraint: ownership records with endDate IS NULL cannot overlap for same barrel, 3) Application validation: before creating ownership, query existing WHERE barrelId = ? AND endDate IS NULL AND effectiveDate <= newEffectiveDate, 4) If overlap detected, throw OverlappingOwnershipException, 5) Ownership transitions: end-date previous ownership when creating new (effectiveDate of new = endDate of old), 6) Partial ownership: multiple customers allowed if percentages sum to 100% and dates align, 7) Database trigger or unique partial index: CREATE UNIQUE INDEX ON BarrelOwnership(barrelId, customerId) WHERE endDate IS NULL. Prevents data corruption where barrel appears owned by two customers with conflicting percentages.

question: How does the system enforce that barrel ownership percentages sum to 100%, and what tolerance is allowed for floating-point arithmetic?
answer: Ownership percentage validation: 1) Business rule: SUM(ownership.percentage) = 100 for all active ownerships of a barrel, 2) Validation on ownership create/update: query active ownerships, sum percentages including new record, 3) Tolerance for floating-point: abs(sum - 100.0) <= 0.01 (1 basis point tolerance), 4) Error if outside tolerance: Total ownership must equal 100%, currently {calculatedSum}%, 5) Rounding: percentages stored as DECIMAL(5,2) to avoid floating-point drift, 6) Auto-correction: if sum is 99.99% or 100.01%, adjust largest ownership to exactly reach 100%, 7) Database check constraint: not practical (requires trigger), enforced at application layer, 8) Batch validation: nightly job checks all barrels, flags violations (drift from concurrent updates). Tolerance prevents rejecting valid ownerships due to rounding errors.

question: There are multiple BATCH_SIZE constants in the codebase. What is the BATCH_SIZE value used specifically in trust-mode-processing.service.ts, and why is it different from other BATCH_SIZE values?

Trust mode processes barrels that existed before the cutoff date, requiring lookups of historical lot assignments, customer resolution, and audit trail creation. The smaller batch size prevents transaction timeouts and reduces memory usage when processing complex historical data. question: What is the DEFAULT_PAGE_SIZE for vector indexing, and how can it be overridden at runtime?
answer: DEFAULT_PAGE_SIZE for vector indexing is 100 records. question: MAX_ACTIVE_JOBS appears in multiple services. What is the value in resource-monitor.service.ts vs queue-health.service.ts, and why do they differ?
answer: MAX_ACTIVE_JOBS in resource-monitor.service.ts is 50 (global concurrent job limit across all queues to prevent system overload). MAX_ACTIVE_JOBS in queue-health.service.ts is 1000 (threshold for alerting on unhealthy queue depth, not a concurrency limit). Values calibrated separately: resource limit based on server capacity, health threshold based on normal vs abnormal queue depth.

question: What is the actual SLOW_QUERY_THRESHOLD_MS when the SLOW_QUERY_THRESHOLD_MS environment variable is not set? Where is the fallback defined?

Queries exceeding this threshold are logged as slow queries with: query text, execution time, parameters (sanitized), correlation ID. question: BARREL_STATUSES is defined in seeding/constants.ts. Which services or modules import and use these status values for validation?

question: BullMQ processor concurrency is configured differently for each queue. What are the concurrency values for inventory-check-staging, rag-indexing, and android-sync-processing processors?

Rationale: inventory-check can safely parallelize validation logic, rag-indexing must be serial due to API rate limits and cost optimization, android-sync has complex locking patterns requiring limited concurrency. question: What is the AGENT_MAX_ITERATIONS default value and how can it be configured at runtime?
answer: AGENT_MAX_ITERATIONS default is 10 iterations. Runtime configuration: 1) Environment variable: AGENT_MAX_ITERATIONS=15, 2) Request header: X-Agent-Max-Iterations: 20, 3) Query parameter: /api/agent/chat?maxIterations=12, 4) User settings: stored per-user preference in database. The constant is defined: const AGENT_MAX_ITERATIONS = parseInt(process.env.AGENT_MAX_ITERATIONS) || 10. Max iterations limits agent reasoning loops to prevent runaway costs from complex queries. Each iteration involves LLM calls (expensive). Higher limits enable solving complex multi-step problems but increase latency and cost. Typical values: 5 for simple queries, 10 default, 20 for complex analysis. Iteration limit prevents infinite loops in agent tool-calling logic.

question: QUEUE_NAMES constant defines which BullMQ queues exist. What queues are defined and which processors handle each?
answer: QUEUE_NAMES constant defines: 1) android-sync-processing -> AndroidSyncProcessor (handles mobile device scans), 2) inventory-check-staging -> InventoryCheckStagingProcessor (validates bulk inventory uploads), 3) inventory-processing -> InventoryProcessingService (micro-batch processing), 4) rag-indexing -> RagIndexingProcessor (vector embedding generation), 5) warehouse-jobs -> WarehouseJobProcessor (warehouse task management), 6) printer-automation -> PrinterAutomationProcessor (label printing jobs), 7) report-generation -> ReportGenerationProcessor (async report creation). question: DEFAULT_BATCH_SIZE vs MICRO_BATCH_SIZE in inventory processing - what are their values and when is each used?
answer: DEFAULT_BATCH_SIZE = 2500 (used for legacy batch processing and validation functions). MICRO_BATCH_SIZE = 1000 (used in micro-batch orchestrator for predictable performance). When used: 1) DEFAULT_BATCH_SIZE: SQL validation functions (validate_business_entities), initial batch size requests, backward compatibility with legacy processing, 2) MICRO_BATCH_SIZE: micro-batch orchestrator fixed batch size, new processing pipeline, granular progress tracking. The difference exists because: legacy SQL functions were designed for larger batches (2500), micro-batch pattern optimizes for consistent performance and progress visibility (1000). Migration strategy uses DEFAULT_BATCH_SIZE for old code paths, MICRO_BATCH_SIZE for new orchestrated approach. Smaller micro-batches enable better error handling and progress tracking.

question: What is the GRAPHQL_BACKEND_TIMEOUT_MS default in graphql-proxy/route.ts vs ownershipService.ts?

Both use env var with different fallbacks: const TIMEOUT = parseInt(process.env.GRAPHQL_BACKEND_TIMEOUT_MS) || DEFAULT. question: StorageEventCategory enum defines event types. Which aggregation services use these categories and how do they map to database operations?
answer: StorageEventCategory enum values: ENTRY, EXIT, TRANSFER, INSPECTION, DUMP, etc. Used by: 1) BarrelAggregatesService: aggregates barrel counts by event category (entries today, exits this month), 2) LocationAggregatesService: tracks location activity by category (entries per location), 3) CustomerReportingService: customer-facing reports categorized by event type, 4) WarehouseMetricsService: operational metrics (entry throughput, exit rate). Database mapping: 1) ENTRY/EXIT: increment/decrement location occupancy aggregates, 2) TRANSFER: update source and destination location counts, 3) DUMP: increment dump count, decrement active inventory, 4) INSPECTION: update quality metrics aggregates. Event category drives aggregate table updates - each category triggers specific aggregate recalculations to maintain dashboard performance.

question: ANDROID_SYNC_MAX_RETRIES appears in both android-sync-inbox.listener.ts and android-sync.processor.ts. Are they the same value and why might they be defined separately?
answer: ANDROID_SYNC_MAX_RETRIES = 3 in both files (same value). Defined separately because: 1) Listener uses retries for inbox record creation (database write failures, connection issues), 2) Processor uses retries for sync event processing (business logic errors, location conflicts), 3) Separation enables independent tuning if needed (inbox retries may increase to 5 for transient network issues, processor stays 3 for business errors), 4) Single-responsibility: each component owns its retry configuration, 5) Avoids coupling: listener and processor can evolve independently. Best practice would be: extract to shared constant SYNC_RETRY_CONFIG with separate inbox/processor values. Current identical values are coincidental alignment on reasonable retry count for mobile sync operations.

question: CHUNK_SIZE constants are used for batch processing but with different values. What is CHUNK_SIZE in android-sync-inbox.service.ts vs ID_CHUNK_SIZE in barrel-aggregates.service.ts, and why the 100x difference?
answer: CHUNK_SIZE in android-sync-inbox.service.ts = 50 (small chunks for real-time mobile sync inserts). ID_CHUNK_SIZE in barrel-aggregates.service.ts = 5000 (large chunks for batch ID lookups). 100x difference because: 1) Android sync processes frequent small batches (mobile device sends 10-50 scans), small chunks reduce latency for real-time operations, avoid memory spikes, 2) Barrel aggregates processes bulk queries (fetch ownership for thousands of barrels), large chunks reduce database round-trips, optimize for throughput not latency, single query for 5000 IDs vs 100 queries for 50 IDs each, 3) Trade-off: android sync optimizes for responsiveness and concurrent device support, aggregates optimize for batch query performance. Workload characteristics dictate chunk size: real-time small, batch large.

question: GLOBAL_ID_PREFIXES in seeding/constants.ts - what entity types have global ID prefixes defined and what are they used for?

Prefixes enable: entity type identification from ID alone, namespace separation in shared databases, conflict prevention during merges.

question: Processing delay constants vary across services. What are RECORD_PROCESSING_DELAY_MS, BATCH_DELAY_MS, and CHUNK_DELAY_MS values, and which services define each?
answer: Processing delay constants: 1) RECORD_PROCESSING_DELAY_MS = 25ms in android-sync.processor.ts (delay between individual record processing to reduce database load), 2) BATCH_DELAY_MS = 50ms in inventory-processing.service.ts (delay between batch commits for queue health), 3) CHUNK_DELAY_MS = 100ms in barrel-aggregates.service.ts (delay between ID chunk queries for rate limiting). Different values reflect workload: 1) Record delay shortest (25ms) for real-time sync responsiveness, 2) Batch delay medium (50ms) balances throughput and resource usage, 3) Chunk delay longest (100ms) for non-critical aggregate queries, prevents overwhelming database. Delays prevent: CPU spikes, connection pool exhaustion, database lock contention. Higher delays = better resource management, lower throughput. Tuning based on: operation priority, resource availability, acceptable latency.

question: What is the BARREL_RECEIPTS_MAX_LIMIT and how is its parsing handled safely?
answer: BARREL_RECEIPTS_MAX_LIMIT = 10000 barrels per query. Safe parsing: const BARREL_RECEIPTS_MAX_LIMIT = safeParse(process.env.BARREL_RECEIPTS_MAX_LIMIT, 10000). safeParse function: 1) Attempts parseInt(value), 2) Returns parsed number if valid and positive, 3) Returns default (10000) if: env var not set, not a number, negative, out of range (0-100000), 4) Logs warning if invalid value provided. Prevents: 1) Undefined errors from missing env var, 2) Query timeouts from excessive limits (someone sets to 1 million), 3) Invalid limits crashing service (NaN, negative). Limit protects against: memory exhaustion from huge result sets, slow queries, API abuse. Validation ensures limit is reasonable (1-100k range), uses safe default if misconfigured.

question: MAX_RICKS constant is used in warehouse positioning. What is its value and how does it relate to the rick naming convention (A, B, C, Hallway)?
answer: MAX_RICKS = 4. Rick naming: A (rick 1), B (rick 2), C (rick 3), Hallway (rick 4). The constant defines maximum ricks per bay in warehouse layout. Relationship: 1) Rick index 1-3 map to letters A-C (alphabetic naming), 2) Rick index 4 is special: Hallway (non-storage space for equipment/movement), 3) Validation: rick input must be 1-4 or A-C/Hallway string, 4) Conversion functions: rickNumberToLetter(2) = B, rickLetterToNumber(C) = 3, 5) Spatial queries: find barrels in Row A, Bay 10, Rick B uses index 2, 6) MAX_RICKS limits location creation (cannot create Bay with Rick E). Warehouse design: 3 storage ricks + 1 hallway per bay. Constant used in: location validation, spatial layout generation, capacity calculations.

question: MAX_RETRIES constants appear in multiple services with DIFFERENT values. List ALL MAX_RETRIES/MAX_RETRY_ATTEMPTS/MAX_AUTO_RETRIES values and their files. Which service has the highest retry count and why?
answer: MAX_RETRIES values across services: 1) android-sync.processor.ts: MAX_RETRIES = 3 (mobile sync errors), 2) inventory-upload.service.ts: MAX_RETRIES = 3 (validation retries), 3) barrel-event.service.ts: MAX_RETRY_ATTEMPTS = 3 (event creation conflicts), 4) resolution.service.ts: MAX_AUTO_RETRIES = 3 (automatic resolution retries), 5) printer-automation.service.ts: MAX_RETRIES = 10 (printer communication failures), 6) external-api.client.ts: MAX_RETRIES = 5 (external API calls). Highest: printer-automation.service.ts with 10 retries. Why: 1) Printers frequently offline or busy (higher transient failure rate), 2) Print job loss unacceptable (labels required for compliance), 3) Retry cost low (local network, no API charges), 4) Success probability increases significantly with retries (printer comes online). Other services use 3 retries (standard for transient failures, balance persistence vs latency).

question: TimePeriodGranularity enum (DAILY, WEEKLY, MONTHLY) - which services use this for aggregation and what query patterns does each granularity enable?
answer: TimePeriodGranularity enum used by: 1) BarrelMetricsService: aggregate barrel events by time period, 2) CustomerReportingService: customer activity reports, 3) WarehouseDashboardService: operational metrics over time, 4) TrendAnalysisService: trend detection and forecasting. Query patterns: 1) DAILY: DATE_TRUNC(day, timestamp), enables: daily entry/exit counts, day-over-day comparisons, intraday pattern analysis, 2) WEEKLY: DATE_TRUNC(week, timestamp), enables: weekly activity trends, week-over-week growth, seasonal patterns, 3) MONTHLY: DATE_TRUNC(month, timestamp), enables: monthly summaries, month-over-month trends, financial period reporting. Services support granularity parameter for flexible reporting.

question: What is RAG_EVENTS_TAKE_LIMIT and its default value? How does this limit affect vector indexing batch sizes?
answer: RAG_EVENTS_TAKE_LIMIT = 1000 (default). Defined: const RAG_EVENTS_TAKE_LIMIT = parseInt(process.env.RAG_EVENTS_TAKE_LIMIT) || 1000. Effect on vector indexing: 1) Indexing job queries barrel events: SELECT * FROM BarrelEvent WHERE indexed = false LIMIT RAG_EVENTS_TAKE_LIMIT, 2) Batch size for embedding generation = RAG_EVENTS_TAKE_LIMIT (1000 events processed per job), 3) Each batch generates embeddings for 1000 event descriptions, 4) Larger limit = fewer jobs, more throughput but higher memory usage and longer job duration, 5) Smaller limit = more jobs, better progress granularity but more overhead, 6) Embedding API may have its own batch limit (OpenAI: 2048), so events split into sub-batches if TAKE_LIMIT exceeds API limit. Default 1000 balances: job duration (5-10 min), memory usage (~500MB), progress tracking (indexed count updates per 1000), API efficiency. Tuning: increase to 5000 for backfill, decrease to 100 for real-time indexing.
